# COMP110 Continuous Improvement Analysis


## Project Overview


For this project, we analyzed whether adding short pre-lecture videos could improve the learning experience for beginner students in COMP110. We focused on students with little to no prior programming experience and examined three variables from the survey data: course pace, pre-lecture videos, and student understanding.


## Analysis Summary


Our analysis looked at whether students’ ratings of pre-lecture videos and course pace were related to their reported understanding. We filtered the data to focus on beginner students because they are the group most likely to benefit from additional support before lecture.


The first scatterplot compares course pace and understanding. The points are spread out, so there is not a very clear relationship between pace and understanding.


<img src="/azizahakimov.github.io-ex09/static/imgs/IMG_4349.png" alt="Scatterplot of pace and understanding" width="500"/>


The second scatterplot compares pre-lecture videos and understanding. This graph also does not show a strong or obvious relationship because the points are spread across many values.


<img src="/azizahakimov.github.io-ex09/static/imgs/IMG_5578.png" alt="Scatterplot of pre-lecture videos and understanding" width="500"/>


The third visualization shows the average understanding score for each pre-lecture video rating. This graph shows a slight downward trend, where higher ratings of pre-lecture videos are associated with slightly lower average understanding.


<img src="/azizahakimov.github.io-ex09/static/imgs/IMG_7052.png" alt="Bar chart of pre-lecture videos and understanding" width="500"/>


## Conclusion


Based on our analysis, the data provides some support for the idea that pre-lecture videos could improve the learning experience for beginner students, but the results are not strongly conclusive.


The scatterplots show that there is not a very strong or clear relationship between pre-lecture video ratings and understanding, or between course pace and understanding. The points are fairly spread out, which suggests that these factors alone may not fully explain differences in student understanding. However, the cat plot shows a slight downward trend, where higher ratings of pre-lecture videos are associated with slightly lower average understanding. This could suggest that students who are struggling may be the ones who feel pre-lecture videos would be useful to them.


Based on our analysis, we recommend a trial of short pre-lecture videos in the coming semester. By trying it for one semester, COMP110 professors could see if it truly makes a difference. The videos may be especially beneficial for students with less programming experience who want to better understand key concepts before class.


Implementing pre-class videos does come with trade-offs. It could increase the workload for students, which may make them feel overwhelmed. It would also create more work for professors because they would need to create and maintain high-quality videos.


For next steps, collecting more data about how students interact with video lessons would be helpful. For example, future surveys could ask how often students actually watch pre-lecture videos, how much time they spend on them, and whether they feel the videos improve their comprehension.


Overall, while the current data does not show a strong effect or clear need for pre-lecture videos, it does suggest that they may still be valuable for certain students, especially those who are new to programming.
